parent.policy_statement: Near-term climate action priorities for transportation.
parent.verbatim_text: Guided by the 2013 Climate Action Plan and a review of recent emissions trends and climate actions, we have identified a suite of near-term priority actions, which will help the City remain on track to meet our climate protection goals. The actions are presented below and summarized in Table 1.
parent.extraction_rationale: Introduces a set of near-term priority actions followed by distinct action items under the TRANSPORTATION heading.

subs.policy_statement: Develop and release a strategy to address congestion and transportation emissions through pricing, coupled with investments in expanded transit and electrification in underserved communities.
subs.verbatim_text: Announce that the City will develop and release a strategy to address congestion and transportation emissions through pricing, coupled with investments in expanded transit and electrification in underserved communities.
subs.parent_policy_name: Near-term climate action priorities for transportation.
subs.extraction_rationale: Specific planned intervention (pricing strategy) with linked investment focus; presented as an action item under the TRANSPORTATION near-term priorities.

subs.policy_statement: Pass a new electric vehicle readiness ordinance in 2018 to ensure new construction or renovation of parking structures is built with electric vehicle infrastructure.
subs.verbatim_text: Pass a new electric vehicle readiness ordinance in 2018, which will ensure new construction or renovation of parking structures is built with electric vehicle infrastructure.
subs.parent_policy_name: Near-term climate action priorities for transportation.
subs.section_header: Electric vehicle readiness ordinance for new construction
subs.extraction_rationale: Clear commitment with a deadline (2018) to pass an ordinance.

subs.policy_statement: Release a map of optimal distribution of charging infrastructure in 2018 and use it to guide public and private investment with a focus on equitable distribution.
subs.verbatim_text: Release map of optimal distribution of charging infrastructure in 2018. The map will guide public and private investment in the network, based on a set of criteria, with a specific focus on equitable distribution
subs.parent_policy_name: Near-term climate action priorities for transportation.
subs.extraction_rationale: Time-bound deliverable (2018 map) and specified intended use (guiding investment/equity).

subs.policy_statement: Work with stakeholders to create recommendations for electrifying shared/for-hire fleets, determine a year after which all such vehicles must be electric, and prepare to permit only electric vehicles for for-hire use.
subs.verbatim_text: The city will work with these stakeholders to create recommendations on how to electrify shared vehicle fleets, determine a year after which all such vehicles must be electric, and prepare for the City to begin permitting only electric vehicles for for-hire use.
subs.parent_policy_name: Near-term climate action priorities for transportation.
subs.section_header: Ride share and taxi fleet electrification
subs.extraction_rationale: Specific regulatory preparation (permitting only EVs) and defined planning outputs; listed as a near-term action.

subs.policy_statement: Create a new Green Fleet Action Plan by the end of 2018 to accelerate electrification of the municipal fleet and phase out fossil fuel use in municipal vehicles.
subs.verbatim_text: Create a new Green Fleet Action Plan, by the end of 2018, to accelerate the electrification of the municipal fleet and phase out fossil fuel use in municipal vehicles.
subs.parent_policy_name: Near-term climate action priorities for transportation.
subs.extraction_rationale: Clear time-bound commitment (end of 2018) to create a new plan with defined objectives.

parent.policy_statement: Near-term climate action priorities to assess GHG emissions impacts of City decisions.
parent.verbatim_text: The City of Seattle does not current have a city-wide policy for tracking the carbon impacts of policies or projects, which limits our ability to fully understand the cost or savings related to carbon emissions.
parent.extraction_rationale: This heading introduces a discrete action item immediately below; treated as a parent for the listed action.

subs.policy_statement: Issue an Executive Order directing City departments to assess the GHG emissions impact of City plans, policies, and major investments, with OSE to define scope and method by October 2018.
subs.verbatim_text: Issue an Executive Order directing City departments to assess the GHG emissions impact of City plans, policies, and major investments. The Mayor will direct the Office of Sustainability & Environment to work with departments to define the types of projects to assess, the assessment method, and how to account for the broader costs of carbon, by October 2018.
subs.parent_policy_name: Near-term climate action priorities to assess GHG emissions impacts of City decisions.
subs.section_header: Assess GHG Emissions Impact of City decisions
subs.extraction_rationale: Binding mechanism (Executive Order) plus a dated implementation milestone (October 2018).

parent.policy_statement: Near-term climate action priorities for buildings.
parent.verbatim_text: To accomplish these goals, policies will first aim to spur actions through incentives and other measures, as well as leading efforts in City-owned buildings, with specific requirements when needed.
parent.extraction_rationale: BUILDINGS section presents multiple distinct actions underneath it (code, pilots, standards, utility programs, municipal targets, conversions), fitting a parent grouping.

subs.policy_statement: Pursue state legislation to enable a tiered residential energy code, making it a City priority in the 2019 legislative session.
subs.verbatim_text: Legislation to allow for tiered energy codes will be a priority for the City of Seattle in the 2019 legislative session.
subs.parent_policy_name: Near-term climate action priorities for buildings.
subs.section_header: Washington State tiered residential energy code
subs.extraction_rationale: Explicit commitment to prioritize legislation in a specific session (2019).

subs.policy_statement: Create a new pilot program offering additional height and floor area incentives for up to 20 major renovations in exchange for deep reductions including no fossil fuels for heating, and adjust related incentives/penalties consistent with the 2030 Challenge.
subs.verbatim_text: A new pilot will offer additional height and floor area incentives for up to 20 major renovations in urban centers outside the International District. Projects would receive the incentives in exchange for cutting energy and water use well below code, including no use of fossil fuels for heating.
subs.parent_policy_name: Near-term climate action priorities for buildings.
subs.section_header: Establish new 2030 Challenge pilot for 20 upgraded, high performing projects by 2025
subs.extraction_rationale: Specific pilot intervention with a numeric cap (20 projects) and defined performance exchange.

subs.policy_statement: Provide programs and incentives to improve efficiency and reduce emissions in commercial and multifamily buildings, backed by a minimum performance standard beginning in 2030.
subs.verbatim_text: Provide programs and incentives to spur improved energy efficiency and reduced carbon emissions, backed by a minimum performance standard beginning in 2030.
subs.parent_policy_name: Near-term climate action priorities for buildings.
subs.section_header: Incentive-driven 2030 performance standards for commercial and multifamily buildings
subs.extraction_rationale: Defines a future binding mechanism (minimum performance standard) with a start date (2030) supported by interim programs.

subs.policy_statement: Seattle City Light will pilot Energy Efficiency as a Service (EEaS) as part of whole-building programs to enable deeper energy efficiency in commercial buildings.
subs.verbatim_text: Seattle City Light will pilot Energy Efficiency as a Service (EEaS), which is explicitly designed to help participants overcome the split incentive barrier in commercial buildings
subs.parent_policy_name: Near-term climate action priorities for buildings.
subs.section_header: City Light Whole Building Pay for Performance (P4P) Programs
subs.extraction_rationale: Explicit commitment to pilot a named utility program (EEaS).

subs.policy_statement: Double the City’s budget allocation from 2021–2025 and target an overall 40% energy and carbon emissions reduction in municipal buildings by 2025.
subs.verbatim_text: Building on existing efforts to reduce energy use 20 percent by 2020, the City will double its budget allocation from 2021 to 2025, and set a new target to achieve an overall 40 percent energy and carbon emissions reduction in municipal buildings by 2025.
subs.parent_policy_name: Near-term climate action priorities for buildings.
subs.section_header: Improve municipal building energy efficiency and reduce carbon emissions by 2025
subs.extraction_rationale: Resource allocation (double budget) plus quantified target and deadline (40% by 2025).

subs.policy_statement: OSE will propose recommendations to accelerate converting 18,000 homes from oil heat to electric heat pumps, including financing for low-income residents and public/private incentives.
subs.verbatim_text: OSE will propose recommendations to the mayor aimed at accelerating the transition of 18,000 homes from heating with oil to an electric heat pump, and adequately serving those unable to finance the switch alone. Recommendations will include a financing plan to support low-income residents, as well as a combination of public and private incentives to spur action.
subs.parent_policy_name: Near-term climate action priorities for buildings.
subs.extraction_rationale: Defined action with a quantified scale (18,000 homes) and specified program elements (financing/incentives).

individual.policy_statement: Seattle adopted a community goal to become carbon neutral by 2050 and directed creation of a plan to meet the goal (Resolution 31312).
individual.verbatim_text: In 2011, the Mayor and City Council adopted a bold climate protection goal for our community to become carbon neutral by 2050 and directed the creation of a plan to meet the goal (Resolution 31312).
individual.extraction_rationale: Formal city goal and directive via cited resolution; clear target year (2050) and governance action.

individual.policy_statement: Seattle City Council affirmed Seattle’s commitment to the Paris Agreement goals and directed OSE to identify actions needed to help limit warming to 1.5°C (Resolution 31757).
individual.verbatim_text: the City Council adopted Resolution 31757, affirming Seattle's commitment to the goals established in the Paris Agreement, and directing the Office of Sustainability & Environment (OSE) to identify the actions necessary to do our part to limit warming to 1.5 degrees Celsius.
individual.extraction_rationale: Cited resolution with explicit directive to a city office and a defined climate objective (1.5°C).

individual.policy_statement: Seattle’s Energy Benchmarking Program requires large nonresidential and multifamily buildings to track and annually report energy performance, and makes reported data publicly available.
individual.verbatim_text: Adopted in 2010, Seattle's Energy Benchmarking Program (SMC 22.920) requires owners of nonresidential and multi-family buildings (20,000 square feet or larger) to track energy performance and annually report to the City. The policy was updated in 2016 to make reported data publicly available to further increase awareness of building energy use and support real estate market transformation.
individual.extraction_rationale: Binding mechanism (SMC code) with defined applicability threshold and reporting requirement.

individual.policy_statement: Seattle’s Building Tune-Ups Ordinance requires large commercial buildings to identify low/no-cost operations and maintenance improvements to improve energy and water efficiency, with phased compliance starting in early 2019.
individual.verbatim_text: Adopted in 2016, the Building Tune-Ups Ordinance (SMC 22.930) requires commercial buildings 50,000 square feet or larger to identifying low- or no-cost building operations and maintenance improvements to improve energy and water efficiency. Compliance deadlines will be phased in by building size, beginning in early 2019.
individual.extraction_rationale: Binding ordinance with clear building-size threshold and compliance timeline.

individual.policy_statement: The City committed to meeting the Building Tune-Ups deadline for City buildings one year earlier than required for private owners (Resolution 31652).
individual.verbatim_text: To demonstrate leadership, build capacity in the industry, and help reduce compliance costs, the city has committed (Resolution 31652) to meeting the Building Tune-Ups deadline one year earlier than required for private owners.
individual.extraction_rationale: Formal commitment referenced to a resolution and a specific accelerated compliance requirement.

individual.policy_statement: Seattle is implementing the Building Tune-Up Accelerator Program to incentivize early compliance with the Tune-Ups Ordinance for mid-size buildings (50,000–100,000 sq ft).
individual.verbatim_text: With funding from a Department of Energy grant, the City is implementing the Building Tune-Up Accelerator Program providing incentives for early compliance with the Tune-Ups Ordinance. The Accelerator is a voluntary program for owners of mid-size buildings, those 50,000-100,000 square feet, to conduct a tune-up that meets the Seattle Building Tune-Up requirements in advance of when their building is required to comply (2020 & 2021).
individual.extraction_rationale: Specific implemented program with defined eligibility and compliance-acceleration years.

individual.policy_statement: Seattle’s Oil Heated Home Conversion program provides incentives for homeowners to replace oil heating systems with electric heat pumps, converting 200 homes per year.
individual.verbatim_text: The Oil Heated Home Conversion program provides incentives for homeowners to replace oil heating systems with clean, efficient, electric heat pumps. By partnering with a heat pump distributor, who provides matching incentives, the program is converting 200 homes per year
individual.extraction_rationale: Concrete incentive program with a quantified annual conversion rate.

individual.policy_statement: Seattle’s 2015 commercial energy code includes emissions-related criteria requiring either non-fossil-fuel-based heating or more efficient windows.
individual.verbatim_text: The 2015 SEC includes criteria that address emissions, by requiring either non-fossil fuel-based heating or more efficient windows.
individual.extraction_rationale: Binding code requirement specifying compliance pathways tied to emissions.

individual.policy_statement: Seattle’s Priority Green program provides expedited permitting for projects achieving green building certification and meeting minimum energy efficiency and other criteria.
individual.verbatim_text: Seattle Department of Construction and Inspections provides expedited permitting for projects achieving green building certification and meeting minimum energy efficiency (15 percent better than code), water conservation, and indoor air quality criteria.
individual.extraction_rationale: Defined city program using permitting incentives with explicit performance thresholds.

individual.policy_statement: Seattle’s Living Building Pilot Program provides additional height and floor area for up to 20 projects meeting specified green building criteria including no on-site fossil-fuel combustion and 25% better energy efficiency than code.
individual.verbatim_text: The Living Building Pilot Program provides additional height and floor area for up to 20 projects that meet more extensive green building criteria, which includes achieving Living Building Petal Certification (with no on-site combustion of fossil-fuels), plus energy efficiency 25 percent better than code, and greywater and/or rainwater used for all non-potable water needs
individual.extraction_rationale: Specific pilot program with a cap (up to 20 projects) and defined technical requirements.

individual.policy_statement: Seattle updated its Sustainable Building Policy for City-owned buildings to require LEED Gold certification and expected 15% better energy efficiency than current code for new construction and major renovations.
individual.verbatim_text: In 2011, the City updated its Sustainable Building Policy for City-owned buildings covering new construction and major renovations. In addition to the requirement to achieve LEED Gold certification, projects are expected to be 15 percent more energy efficient than the current code.
individual.extraction_rationale: Named city policy with explicit certification and efficiency expectations.

individual.policy_statement: Seattle adopted a Resource Conservation Management Plan with a goal to reduce energy use across City-owned buildings by 20% by 2020 (2008 baseline).
individual.verbatim_text: In 2013, the City adopted a Resource Conservation Management Plan (Resolution 31491), with the goal to achieve a 20 percent reduction in energy use across City-owned buildings by 2020 (2008 baseline).
individual.extraction_rationale: Formal plan with quantified target, deadline, and baseline.

individual.policy_statement: Seattle amended the Seattle Electrical Code to require new parking to be built to facilitate future electric vehicle charging, including electrical capacity for one EV charging station per parking space.
individual.verbatim_text: In 2017, the Seattle Electrical Code was amended to ensure new parking is built to facilitate future electric vehicle charging infrastructure. The code requires adequate electrical capacity to serve one electric vehicle charging station per parking space
individual.extraction_rationale: Binding code amendment with a clear technical requirement.

individual.policy_statement: Seattle City Light will install 20 public fast charging stations through the right-of-way charging pilot program to increase access and improve equitable distribution of charging.
individual.verbatim_text: Through the right of way charging pilot program described above, City Light is installing 20 public fast charging stations to increase access, while also ensuring that charging is distributed more equitably around the city.
individual.extraction_rationale: Concrete implementation action with a quantified infrastructure commitment.

individual.policy_statement: Seattle’s Green Fleet Action Plan guides efforts to reduce municipal fleet emissions 50% by 2025 through electrification, cleaner fuels where needed, efficiency, and procurement standards.
individual.verbatim_text: The Green Fleet Action Plan guides the City's efforts to meet the goal to reduce municipal fleet emissions by 50 percent by 2025 by increasingly electrifying the fleet, using cleaner fuels when electrification is not feasible, increasing efficiency in how vehicles are used, and setting standards for vehicle procurement.
individual.extraction_rationale: Named plan with a quantified target and described mechanisms.
